Row.Text: The free and self-reliant nation forged in the struggle for Purna Swaraj — heir to the subcontinent's ancient learning, its many faiths and tongues marching in a single column under a single greeting: Jai Hind.

The Maurya carved their edicts in stone; the Chola sent their bronzes across the southern seas; through every age of conquest, the learning of the subcontinent endured — its astronomers, its surgeons, its masters of steel. Now that inheritance passes to a free people. Bharat rises — one nation of many faiths and many tongues, its ranks unbroken, its forges lit, its future planned by no hand but its own. Jai Hind!

+{X}%[icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production in settlements with a Production building. {Y}% less War Weariness in your settlements. +{Z}[icon:YIELD_HAPPINESS] Happiness in all settlements for each foreign civilization that founded a settlement you control.

+{X}%[icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production in settlements with a Production building. {Y}% less War Weariness in your settlements. +{Z}[icon:YIELD_HAPPINESS] Happiness in all settlements for each foreign civilization that founded a settlement you control.

+{X}%[icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production in settlements with a Production building. {Y}% less War Weariness in your settlements. +{Z}[icon:YIELD_HAPPINESS] Happiness in all settlements for each foreign civilization that founded a settlement you control.
Unique Bharatiya infantry unit that replaces the Line Infantry. +1[icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength when fighting outside friendly territory.
Tier 2 unique Bharatiya infantry unit that replaces the Rifle Infantry. +1[icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength when fighting outside friendly territory.
Tier 3 unique Bharatiya infantry unit that replaces the Infantry Company. +1[icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength when fighting outside friendly territory.
+1 [icon:NAR_REW_COMBAT] Combat Strength when fighting outside friendly territory.

"Azad Hind Fauj" — the Free India Army. First raised in 1942 from Indian soldiers and expatriates across Southeast Asia and reborn under Subhas Chandra Bose in 1943, the Indian National Army was built as no colonial army had been: its regiments deliberately mixed Hindu, Muslim, and Sikh soldiers in the same ranks, bound by a single greeting — "Jai Hind." In the Rani of Jhansi Regiment, under Captain Lakshmi Swaminathan, it fielded one of the modern world's first all-women infantry units. In 1944 its columns crossed onto Indian soil in Manipur beneath the cry "Dilli Chalo!" — On to Delhi! The march was turned back, but the army won its war in defeat: when three of its officers — a Hindu, a Muslim, and a Sikh, tried together — faced court-martial at the Red Fort in 1945, all India rose in their defense, and within two years India was free. Jai Hind!
Unique Bharatiya quarter, formed when the Yojana Bhavan and the Vaidyashala share a district. +1[icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production for every 3 Population in this settlement.
+1[icon:YIELD_PRODUCTION] Production for every 3 Population in this settlement.

At Haripura in 1938, Subhas Chandra Bose declared that the first task of a free national government would be a commission to plan the reconstruction of India — its "mother industries" foremost. That same year, as Congress president, he convened the National Planning Committee: a nation drafting its economic future nine years before it was free. When freedom came, the idea became the Planning Commission of the new republic, and its twin concerns endured — the strength of the nation's industry and the health of its people, the body politic and the body itself, each built by no hand but Bharat's own. Jai Hind!

"Yojana" — the plan. From the blueprints of the 1938 committee to the Five-Year Plans drafted at Yojana Bhavan in Delhi, free India was built by design: steel, dams, and mother industries raised by the nation's own hand.

The hall of the vaidya, the physician — heir to Sushruta's surgery and to the Ayurvedic revival that made indigenous medicine itself an act of self-reliance: a free people healing and growing by its own ancient learning.
The vow of one's own country: what Bharat wears, burns, and builds, Bharat shall make.
Complete independence, declared in 1930 — no half-freedoms, no dominion under another's crown.
The uncompromising consolidated in 1939: the struggle presses forward, without pause and without retreat.
A free India proclaimed in 1943 before it could be won — first the state was declared, then it was fought into being.
An economy drafted before freedom itself: mother industries, raised by the nation's own hand.

Bharat is the name the subcontinent gave itself long before any mapmaker called it India — a land named in its oldest texts for a legendary king, and understood for millennia not as one state but as one civilization of many kingdoms, faiths, and tongues. From the edicts the Maurya cut into rock, to the bronzes the Chola cast and carried across the southern seas, to the astronomers who measured the heavens and the surgeons who set down their craft, that civilization endured through every age of conquest, absorbing conquerors rather than being erased by them.

What it had never been, in the modern age, was free. The struggle for Purna Swaraj — complete independence — gathered every strand of a vast and quarrelsome people: the spinner and the strategist, the lawyer and the soldier. Subhas Chandra Bose gave one wing of that struggle its sharpest edge, raising an army whose regiments deliberately mixed Hindu, Muslim, and Sikh under a single salute, and convening, as early as 1938 and as Congress president, a National Planning Committee that dared to design the economy of a nation that did not yet exist. His conviction was simple and total: a free people must be able to feed, arm, and heal themselves by their own hand.

The Bharat of this age is that conviction made real: a self-reliant republic that turns an ancient inheritance of learning toward modern purpose, that plans its own forges and its own clinics, and that draws its strength not from a single creed but from the many peoples who march in one column beneath one flag. Jai Hind.
Subhas Chandra Bose fought to forge a free and self-reliant Bharat — the nation this civilization makes real.
From the Maurya's stone edicts to a free republic — the same civilization, come at last into its own hands.
The Chola carried the subcontinent's craft across the seas; Bharat gathers that inheritance into one self-reliant nation.
